data.title: Essential Utilities: When Boring Becomes Profitable
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Utility sector has always been the go-to place for investors who search for predictable returns.

For perspective, demand is always constant because people need water, wastewater, and gas. Besides that, the regulation allows the company to earn reliable profits. This whole phenomenon is brought to life by Essential Utilities (WTRG).

Essential supplies water, wastewater, and gas to millions and expand as a result of smart investments and approved rate increases. A case in point is Q3, which recorded a significant growth in its water and gas segments.

Moreover, each dollar Essential invests in its systems today will translate into revenue in the future. The management continues to increase dividends while investing in the business to ensure stable growth.

In addition, the merger with American Water Works Company (AWK) deserves attention. Naturally, merging two large companies has some risks, but if it succeeds, the growth will accelerate faster than the usual rate.

Furthermore, WTRG stock is trading at a fair value relative to the sector median. The market prices in stable performance but underestimates the upside from the merger and future rate increments.

Overall, WTRG is a 'buy' right now and has the potential to provide consistent growth with upside.

Q3 2025: Consistency Still Wins

Essential’s Q3 performance is commendable as it performed across the board.

It generated $477 million of revenue, a rise of almost 10% YOY. Profits jumped as well. Net income increased by an incredible 32.7% annually and clocked in at $92 million, or $0.33 per share.

The water business did its share of heavy lifting and contributed a whopping $364 million in revenue, an increase of nearly 9%. The gas segment came in next at $108 million, up over 12% YOY.

This growth was a result of sanctioned rate upgrades rather than mere usage changes. Meanwhile, Essential managed to keep the costs down and it worked. This is what enabled the margins to appear exactly where you would expect for a regulated utility.

Interestingly, the company still invests back into the system. During the initial 9 months of 2025, Essential spent $983 million, and it predicts to invest between $1.4 to $1.5 billion for the entire year.

Moving further ahead, the management has planned $7.8 billion of infrastructure investment between 2025 and 2029. Those investments are important as regulators permit Essential to make a reasonable profit, typically within 8-10% on each new asset added to the rate base.

Consequently, the current expenditure becomes the future revenue. The regulated rate base is anticipated to increase at a rate of 6-8% per year, which drives consistent and predictable growth in earnings. Essential’s CEO Chris Franklin claimed on the Q3 earnings call:

Our business and its economic model have proven resilient through economic booms and downturns, as well as through political and regulatory changes.

Most importantly, Essential's dividend profile is indicative of the same discipline that characterizes the business. The stock provides a forward yield of approximately 3.5% and an annual payout of $1.37/share. It's backed by a payout ratio of 60%, which leaves an opportunity to reinvest and grow in the future.

The dividend has climbed at an impressive 6.6% annual rate over the past half decade, and the company has increased its dividend for 34 consecutive years. The most recent quarterly dividend is at $0.34 per share, which is paid on a regular quarterly basis.

To be honest, this is an overall reliable income source. It is supported by controlled cash flows and a management team that is clearly focused on long-term dividend growth.

Earnings Boosted by Regulation

Regulation continues to work in favor of Essential, and it is reflected in earnings clearly.

The company has already secured good rate increments. The water and wastewater business contributed an estimated $93 million in annual revenue in various states by early November 2025, with gas contributing around $9 million. To add to that, some rate cases are still pending and can possibly generate more revenues if approved.

These wins are important, as rate gains go directly to the bottom line. Each $10 million in new rates can contribute between $0.02 and $0.03 to EPS, which makes each case much more effective than organic growth on its own.

Earnings momentum is strong even prior to counting pending cases. Essential posted full-year 2024 GAAP EPS of $2.17, up 17% YOY, and the management restated 2025 guidance somewhere between $2.07 and $2.11. These perspectives already comprise planned capital spending and known regulatory results. If rest of the cases work their way, the EPS may surpass expectations, demonstrating how regulation acts as an in-built cash flow booster.

In addition to rates, other consistent growth drivers are acquisitions. Essential has increased its water and wastewater rate base by $548 million, adding about 136,000 customers since 2015. In 2025 alone, it made deals that served a little more than 10,000 customers. It has also signed contracts in Pennsylvania and Texas, which can bring approximately 200,000 more, including the massive Delcora sewer purchase.

Although such additions are minor in comparison to the total base, they gradually increase the revenue, FCF and the long-term rate base.

Essential Taps American for Growth

Here’s the big thing: an all-stock merger with American Water.

In the deal, every Essential share will be converted into nearly 0.305 AWK shares, which is a 10% premium. Combined, the company would serve 4.7 million water and wastewater connections and have approximately $29.3 billion in rate base.

Additionally, the key shareholders would be in possession of about 31% of the new company. Hence, they get exposure to the wider footprint and credit profile of AWK. This scale definitely has the potential of increasing regulatory credibility and establishing operating leverage by joint overhead and operations.

Moreover, the management anticipates that the merger will instantly increase EPS and dividends. They also intend to maintain AWK's long-term 7-9% EPS and dividend growth targets. The two companies will keep on paying their existing dividends until the deal is closed, and that adds stability for shareholders.

The merger also comes with complementary advantages. For example, Peoples Gas of Essentials serves 705,000 customers in Pennsylvania and is expanding by more than 10% a year. This brings in a fast-expanding regulated gas business. In the meantime, access to the greater capital base of Americans could reduce the cost of financing new projects. The management also recognizes cross-selling and cost-saving opportunities but they have yet to provide exact numbers on synergy.

Naturally, the merger comes with its risks (as will be discussed in the ‘risks to my thesis’ section of this piece). That said, this merger could be a superb catalyst and can generate value that Essential would have struggled to create alone.

Valuation Reflects Stability While Upside Builds

WTRG stock experienced a decent 12.6% rise over the last year. It began at $34-$35 at the start of 2025, reached just over $40 in October, and is currently trading at around $39.

The stock's plateau at $39 shows the market weighing two large factors, which are pending rate cases and the American Water merger. Investors are indicating that they prefer consistent cash flows as opposed to short-term frenzy. This basically means that the market’s psychology is to reward stability and regulatory certainty, which is precisely what Essential provides.

The future EPS forecasts give a clearer picture. Analysts project $2.12 in 2025 and $2.23 in 2026, with revenue expected to improve from $2.37 billion to $2.44 billion. At the current price, the non-GAAP forward P/E is 18.76x, a bit lower than the sector median, which indicates stable growth. This is where rate base comes in. Every approved investment contributes to the regulated asset base, making 8-10% returns, which compound earnings on a predictable basis.

If Essential keeps implementing its projects, increasing its rate base, and closing small acquisitions, EPS may move toward the upper end of projections. That will give a reasonable base-case price range of $38 to $41 in the upcoming 12 months, with dividends serving as a reliable ground for total returns.

The bull case is based on catalysts that the market is keenly monitoring. If regulators grant more rate cases, EPS can go beyond $2.23. Add that to the American Water merger, and forward P/E would trend in the low-19x range, pushing the stock to $42-44.

This situation indicates not only the earnings increase but also the rewards that come with increased scale and credibility in the market.

Risks

For starters, the regulatory risk is the most critical. Rate-case approvals and cost recoveries are vital in the growth of Essential. Delays or rejections would decrease the estimated revenues, and a loss of $10-$20 million in approved rates would decrease EPS by a few cents. That would have a direct impact on cash flow and the capability of the company to finance capital projects per year or maintain dividend growth.

Speaking of financing, Essential plans to spend $1.4-1.5 billion on infrastructure yearly, with total investments of about $7.8 billion over the period of 2029. FCF might be strained if regulators push back on cost recovery or financing costs increase. Even with approvals, funding such projects either by debt or equity raises interest and dilution risk that may restrain operational flexibility and slow the growth in dividends.

Additionally, the merger with American Water has performance and integration risks. The transaction requires shareholder approval and regulatory approval in various states. Delay and conditions may decrease the expected upside.

The merger of two big utilities is complicated. The systems, workforces, and IT platforms might cause friction and slow execution. Even small setbacks would postpone synergies and subject short-term EPS growth to pressure.

Last but not least, short-term earnings could also be influenced by other factors like extreme weather events, temporary fall in demand, or political resistance against rate increases. Although the company possesses mechanisms to recover costs in the long run, timing gaps may create short-term pressure.

Takeaway

Here’s the truth: WTRG is the type of stock that silently works for you.

It has stable growth, a robust dividend, and a regulation that ensures earnings remain predictable. Moreover, the Q3 results, recent rate wins, and the merger with American Water mean that the management performs well and thinks about long-term growth.

Its stock is priced fairly, and future rate approvals and merger synergies provide it with a meaningful upside. For anyone seeking reliable growth and a consistent income stream, buying WTRG is a prudent choice. It creates value in the long run and lets you focus on all other things without concern.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: Time To Consider An Exit Strategy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Every party has to come to an end. It looks like it is time to assess the post-split-up value of the original AT&T (T) purchase that resulted in the situation now with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD).

Two Strategies

In the case of Warner Bros Discovery, I followed two strategies. I had originally suggested that conservative investors take some profits after the AT&T sale to Warner Bros Discovery and the receipt of Warner Bros Discovery shares. That would have resulted in a roughly 20% profit immediately. For those that happened to keep the shares purchased, I made about 30% plus the dividend paid on my money, keeping the whole deal until now over a four-year period. But the Warner Bros. Discovery shares were up about 25% (with no dividend) very roughly in the post-split time period of four years. While any gain is better than a loss, that 3- to 4-year gain (roughly) was certainly nothing to write home about on the Warner Bros. Discovery hold idea.

However, in both cases, when the companies ran into issues. I also purchased some shares of both later. In the case of Warner Bros Discovery, those shares were purchased at (slightly) less than $11 per share, which made for a very nice gain at current prices. With financially strong companies, this points out the success of the averaging down strategy. But you have to have some faith in the strategy that management chooses when it runs into unexpected issues, or things can go south pretty fast.

Doing this purchase later turned a mediocre at best original return into something decent overall.

Exit Strategies

But now it is time to consider exiting Warner Bros Discovery common shares. The reason for this is the takeover offer by Netflix (NFLX) covered in the last article. The takeover offer essentially caps the appreciation potential in the near future.

Since that article, the offer has been amended to become an all-cash offer. While that is not much of a change, it is what we are dealing with in forming an exit strategy.

Conservative investors may want to sell now. The reason for that is there is now a sure thing. The cash received now can be invested elsewhere for another investment idea (with hopefully good results). Sometimes these deals come undone. Conservative investors may not want to take that risk.

On the other hand, there is a possibility that a bidding war could erupt after a conservative sale is made. Chart Industries (GTLS) received an offer from Baker Hughes (BKR) that was acceptable a fair amount of time after it had accepted another offer. So late offers happen, and management can change its mind.

On the other hand, an investor can choose to hold until the Netflix offer is received, and the remainder of the company business is spun off to holders. This is very roughly 10% to 15% above the current price. Whether it is worth holding until this happens is up to the investor. At what point the stock price climbs close enough that it is worth not holding on anymore is a personal decision.

For me, I am considering selling because I think that at this point it is no longer worth it for me to hold until the whole deal closes and is complete. But that may not be everyone's choice. That makes this now a hold because the future is finite (in the upwards direction) and probably a good deal lower than if the company had stayed independent.

Buying Netflix

There is a possibility that in the future I may well purchase Netflix because I believe that the combination materially improves its profitability model.

However, right now the stock is in a downtrend. I may therefore consider purchasing long-dated calls or even leaps because I do believe that the stock is priced way ahead of what the value will be in the future, given management's guidance of growth in the teens annually for some time now.

Generally, the price-earnings ratio should be one times the growth rate. It now looks like the stock price is beginning to adjust to that, as the stock price is down substantially from its high point.

It is quite possible that the acquisition offer made by Netflix was a wake-up call for the market to realize that the Netflix model needed considerable upgrading to remain independent and compete with the competition that has entered the market.

I believe that Netflix had a serious deficiency in that it basically made money from streaming, whereas the competition had movies, games, and even theme parks (among other things). Therefore, the competition could make money in more than one way off the content. This provided a profitability advantage that Netflix needed.

After the acquisition, the assimilation process will be significant in that the Netflix content needs to become monetized in more than one way in addition to what Warner Bros Discovery is already monetizing.

Netflix management has long stated that the Warner Bros. Discovery management will remain as this is by and large not businesses that Netflix is in (except for streaming). Therefore, it makes sense to allow experienced management to continue and learn from them. That should ensure merger success and make the assimilation process relatively seamless.

Summary

I tend to wait until things calm down whenever there is a merger in the future. Maybe I do not receive the best stock price. But I do follow the Warren Buffett maxim about buying low enough that even a mediocre sale brings about a decent result.

As a result, I have learned the value of being able to average down if that opportunity presents itself. If the stock price heads upwards after a merger, there is always the possibility of averaging up (which can be just as profitable but is far less discussed).

Because of all the possibilities, I consider Warner Bros Discovery a hold even though I likely will sell in the near future. There are too many investor profiles out there for one strategy to make everyone happy. Plus, the future is really unknown. The risk assessment for the situation has got to be a personal evaluation of the possibilities and ownership of what happens.

Risks

The risk largely depends upon the pathway taken in the future. A decision to hold onto the stock until the deal closes risks the deal coming undone and an immediate stock price decline from current levels. I happen to believe in the future of an independent Warner Bros. Discovery, so the potential possibility that the deal comes undone, and the company remains independent is not much of a threat.

On the other hand, sellers now risk that a better offer will come along and they will not have the stock to participate in that better offer if it is accepted by management. Right now, I consider this risk pretty small. But then again, the future is largely unknown.

There are merger conditions that have to be met, and some permissions are needed. These could prove to be insurmountable, which would cause the deal to fall apart.

Overall, though, it looks like things have settled down enough that it is time to plan an exit strategy. Let me know below what you decide.

data.title: Medallion Financial Is Consistently Delivering Strong Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Medallion Financial (MFIN) has been growing its asset base consistently over the last few years and has been earning an excess return over its cost of equity. While close to stable growth, I estimate that Medallion Financial still has a few years to continue earning a premium over its cost of equity. Its net interest margin (the difference between its interest income and interest expense) has generally increased from 6.82% in 2021 to 7.33% in the trailing twelve months (“TTM”) ended September 30, 2025 (the “TTM period”). Its revenue growth minus non-interest expense growth has been in the double digits from 2021 to 2024 (with the exception of 2022, when it was 9.36%) and ended the TTM period at 10.77%. The company also reinstated its quarterly dividend at $0.08 in the first quarter of 2022, after which it increased the quarterly dividend to $0.10 in the fourth quarter of 2023 and increased it again to $0.12 in the second quarter of 2025. MFIN currently has a forward annual payout of $0.48 and a forward yield of 4.58%. Finally, the company has strong insider ownership of approximately 20%.

Company Overview

Medallion Financial is a specialty finance firm that originates and services both consumer loans (recreational vehicle loans and home improvement loans) and commercial loans, as well as also offering loan origination services to fintech strategic partners. It operates primarily through its two subsidiaries, Medallion Bank (MBNKO), which offers consumer loans, and Medallion Capital, which offers commercial loans.

Medallion Bank opened in December 2003 in Salt Lake City, Utah, to support its parent company’s taxicab medallion lending business. They ceased originating taxicab medallion loans in late 2014, and the bank and its parent company have since wound down the vast bulk of that portfolio to only $5.1 million in net assets as of September 30, 2025. The bank now offers consumer loans on recreational vehicles and boats, as well as loans for the purchase of home improvements. Since 2020, the bank has also offered strategic partnerships via banking-as-a-service lending. Medallion Financial is the sole owner of all of Medallion Bank’s common stock. There are currently two preferred classes of stock at the bank, one of which, the Series G preferred stock, has traded publicly as MBNKO since May 22, 2025. I previously covered this security in another article for Seeking Alpha. The other series of preferred stock is the Series E Preferred Stock, of which 26,303 shares with a liquidation amount of $1,000 per share are outstanding with the U.S. Treasury under the Small Business Lending Fund Program.

Medallion Capital handles Medallion Financial’s commercial lending segment, targeting lower middle market companies in the U.S. Investments range from $2.5 million to $6.0 million. Medallion Capital is a Small Business Investment Company (“SBIC”), which provides debt and equity financing to small businesses and startups while being regulated by the U.S. Small Business Administration.

As of September 30, 2025, Medallion Financial’s book value of shareholders’ equity has grown from 2021 at a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 12.07%. Its book value per share is $17.07, and its tangible book value (book value less goodwill and intangible assets) per share is $9.82. Per management, this tangible book value per share should be adjusted upwards by $1.82 if it includes a correlated deferred tax liability associated with goodwill and intangible assets. This would result in an adjusted tangible book value per share of $11.64, well above the levels MFIN is trading at currently.

Ratio Analysis

A look at some historical ratios (calculated using Medallion Financial’s 10-Ks and 10-Qs) gives an indication of how Medallion Financial has performed. A series of ratios on profitability, financial strength, and efficiency, all using figures as reported, are shown in the following table.

Net Interest Margin measures the difference between interest income and interest expenses, with a higher margin being better. Medallion Financial has generally grown its net interest margin from 6.82% in 2021 to 7.33% in the TTM period.

Return on Equity (“ROE”) shows how much net income is generated by the common shareholder’s equity (note, net income is what remains after income attributable to the non-controlling interest is paid out). While this has generally declined over the years reviewed, it was still a respectable 11.31% for the TTM period and higher than its cost of equity. Please note, for consistency with some of the sources used, ROE is calculated using the prior year’s book value of common shareholders’ equity (not the average of the prior year’s and current year’s book value of equity).

Return on Assets (“ROA”) measures net income against total assets of Medallion Financial. This measure also generally declined from 3.29% in 2021 to 1.42% in the TTM period. Please note, for consistency with some of the sources used, ROA is calculated using the prior year’s book value of assets (not the average of the prior year’s and current year’s book value of assets).

The provision for credit losses ratio shows the amount of provisions for credit losses as a percentage of net loans. This number increased from 3.49% in 2021 to 4.68% in the TTM period. The provision for credit losses measure also reduces net income and increases the allowance for credit losses, a contra-asset account on the balance sheet.

Several ratios for financial strength were reviewed, with higher percentages showing greater financial strength. The Tier 1 leverage ratio shows a bank’s core capital to its total assets. Common Equity Tier 1 (“CET1”) risk-based capital ratio shows a bank’s common equity (i.e., exclusive of preferred stock and qualifying minority interests) against its risk-weighted assets. Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's equity capital and disclosed reserves against its total risk-weighted assets. Finally, the total risk-based capital ratio shows the sum of Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital divided by the bank’s risk-weighted assets. All of these measures are significantly above regulatory minimums for all years reviewed, including the TTM period.

The efficiency ratio shows non-interest expenses per dollar of revenue, with a lower ratio being better. This ratio gradually decreased over the years reviewed, from 45.74% in 2021 to 33.34% in the TTM period.

Revenue growth less non-interest expense growth, when positive, shows that revenue is growing faster than expenses, as opposed to a negative ratio, which shows the company is accumulating expenses faster than it is growing revenues. This figure generally declined from 35.85% in 2021 to 10.77% in the TTM period, with a low of 9.36% in 2022.

In summary, all of these ratios show Medallion Financial has historically had solid performance and a consistent ability to more than adequately meet its regulatory capital requirements and still be able to pay out dividends.

Adjustments to Historical Net Income, ROE & ROA

Using Medallion Financial's 10-Ks and 10-Qs, the company’s historical net income, ROE, and ROA from 2021 through the TTM period were reviewed. These figures, including the subtractions from net income after taxes of income attributable to the non-controlling interest (dividends to the two outstanding series of preferred stock at Medallion Bank) and the redemption of non-controlling interests in the TTM period (redemption of the Series F Preferred Stock of Medallion Bank), are shown in the following table.

Historical net income ranged from a low of $35.88 million in 2024 to a high of $55.08 million in 2023, with an average of $45.98 million for all periods. ROE averaged 15.74% across all periods, and ROA averaged 2.18% across all periods.

In the next table, labeled Adjusted Net Income, ROE, & ROA, the historical net income attributable to the common shareholder (as reported) is shown at the top. To better compare historical periods’ net income with the recent material increase in the annual preferred dividends paid out for the newly issued Series G Preferred Stock, I made adjustments to net income attributable to the common shareholder.

Starting with net income attributable to the common shareholder, the historical income attributable to the non-controlling interest was added back for all periods reviewed, and the redemption of a non-controlling interest in the TTM period was added back. I then subtracted from all periods the current annual preferred dividends for the Series E Preferred Stock and subtracted the annual preferred dividends for the new Series G Preferred Stock under adjusted income attributable to the non-controlling interest (Bank Series G).

There were also two factors included in 2021 that are not present in any other year, so these were adjusted out to make 2021’s net income more comparable with the later periods. The $4.63 million gain on extinguishment of debt was subtracted from 2021’s net income, as was the $12.57 million in net sponsorship & race winnings (from a previously owned professional race team disposed of in 2021). I also added back the race team-related expenses of $9.56 million from 2021.

Finally, in December 2024, there was a one-time expense of a civil penalty of $3.0 million due to a settlement agreement with the SEC resolving outstanding litigation. Please see Risks & Other Factors for a more detailed description. I adjusted this one-time penalty expense out of fiscal year 2024 and the TTM period's net income.

These changes resulted in the adjusted net income attributable to the common shareholder, as shown in the following table.

Adjusted net income ranged from a low of $35.58 million in 2024 to a high of $51.78 million in 2023, with an average of $42.94 million across all periods. Adjusted ROE averaged 14.39% across all periods, and adjusted ROA averaged 1.98% across all periods. Adjusted earnings per share (diluted) for the TTM period is $1.91, compared to $1.70 per share (diluted) for the unadjusted net income attributable to the common shareholder.

Excess Return Model

An excess return model calculates the value of a firm by summing capital currently invested and the present value of excess returns that the firm is expected to make in the future. This model is ideal for financial services companies with complex and uncertain cash flows. To accurately calculate the present value of future returns, several assumptions and inputs need to be decided upon.

Given the average adjusted net income of $42.94 million was comparable to the TTM period's adjusted net income of $46.12 million, I used the TTM period's adjusted net income as the base net income for the model and used the book value of equity of $362.39 million at the start of the TTM period (i.e., October 1, 2024) as the base level book value of equity used to calculate an ROE of 12.73%. Adjusted earnings per share for the TTM period (adjusted net income divided by the weighted average common shares outstanding on a diluted basis, or 24.1 million shares) of $1.91 and annual dividends per share of $0.48 were used in the model.

To calculate the discount rate, or cost of equity, I used the Build-Up Method and the Capital Asset Pricing Model (“CAPM”). I selected a beta of 0.68 (provided by Yahoo Finance using 5 years of monthly returns), an equity risk premium of 4.23% for 2025 (as provided by Professor Aswath Damodaran, a professor at Stern School of Business at New York University), and a risk-free rate of 4.275% (the 10-year Treasury note). Given the market capitalization of MFIN is $229.9 million, placing it roughly in the 9th decile of all publicly traded stocks ranked by market capitalization, I also added an estimated small stock risk premium of 2.5% to arrive at a cost of equity of approximately 9.65%.

Since the adjusted net incomes of the review period were relatively similar, I estimated a length of a high-growth period for Medallion Financial of three years, with a starting ROE of 12.73% (as previously described) and the book value of equity of $397.45 million as of September 30, 2025. The retention rate of 74.87% was estimated by taking 1 minus the current dividends per share divided by the current earnings per share.

Over the 3-year high-growth period, I adjusted the ROE downwards gradually to 9.75% in the terminal year. I obtained this ROE from Professor Aswath Damodaran’s research showing an average ROE of 9.75% for regional banks (the closest comparable industry available) as of January 2026. I also adjusted the retention ratio downwards from 74.87% to 30.77% in the terminal year. Finally, I gradually adjusted the cost of equity upwards from 9.65% to 11.01% in the terminal year (driven by increasing the beta from 0.68 to 1.00 at the period of stable growth of the terminal year).

This resulted in excess equity returns of $12.22 million, $7.10 million, and negative $5.85 million for future years one through three. The third year is negative as that year is when the cost of equity of 11.01% exceeds the stable-growth period’s ROE of 9.75%. The terminal value also uses this cost of equity and ROE, resulting in a terminal value of negative $75.26.

The present value of these returns, as discounted using the cumulated cost of equity (which considers the gradual growth in cost of equity), is $11.15 in year one, $5.88 in year two, and negative $60.52 in year three (inclusive of the terminal value). These sum to equal a present value of equity excess returns of negative $43.49, as shown in the following tables.

When added to the $397.45 of equity invested on September 30, 2025, and then divided by the number of shares outstanding (on a diluted basis), it results in an estimated value per share of $14.67, or $4.82 higher than today’s share price of $9.85. This implies an upside of 48.9% from current levels.

Risks & Other Factors

There are some risks to factor into the purchase of MFIN, as detailed in the company’s most recent 10-K. Primary among them is a rapid shift in interest rates. Per its latest 10-K, the market risk of an immediate increase in interest rates of 1% over a one-year period would be a reduction in net income of $2.7 million at December 31, 2024. A decrease in interest rates, while contributive to the value of outstanding loans, could raise reinvestment risk as amounts repaid on existing loans could not be reinvested at the same rates previously obtained. Additionally, the risk of early refinancing of outstanding loans increases as market interest rates decrease. There is a risk that the allowance for credit losses may prove to be insufficient to cover loan losses. There is a risk that financial covenants under debt arrangements could be breached, a default of which could accelerate related and other debt to immediate repayment. Due to its structure as a holding company, Medallion Financial is dependent on cash flows from its subsidiaries to make payments on debts and to fund operations. As stated in the most recent 10-Q (September 30, 2025),

"on December 29, 2021, the SEC filed a civil complaint in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York against the Company and its President and Chief Operating Officer alleging certain violations of the antifraud, books and records, internal controls and anti-touting provisions of the federal securities laws.

...

On May 30, 2025, the Court entered a Final Judgment as to the Company and its President and Chief Operating Officer resolving this litigation. The parties agreed to settle the matter with the SEC, consenting to the entry of the Final Judgment, without admitting or denying the allegations of the SEC complaint. Pursuant to the Final Judgment, among other things, (i) the parties were enjoined from violating specified provisions of the federal securities laws and rules thereunder, (ii) the Company paid a civil penalty of $3,000,000 (which amount was previously accrued in the fourth quarter of 2024) and (iii) the Company agreed to certain compliance-related undertakings."

The company does not use any AI tools or models in critical areas of its business, and it is not aware of any third parties that use AI tools or models in a manner that would materially impact Medallion Financial’s business, but this might change in the future. The company had previously suspended its dividend (during the height of its taxi medallion lending crisis) and only recently brought it back. Finally, the company’s concentration in consumer lending typically carries a risk of loss that is higher than commercial lending and can be adversely affected by an economic downturn. The likelihood of an economic downturn increases substantially when trade wars heat up, as they are currently, or if the independence of the Federal Reserve comes into question.

Conclusion

Given all of the above, I believe the potential upside of MFIN far outweighs its risks. The company has mostly overcome the stigma of its former taxi medallion lending business and has a bright future ahead of it. My estimated value per share of $14.67 using an excess return model reflects that potential upside of 48.9%.

data.title: Antero Resources: Projected FCF Boosted By Recent Transactions
data.description:
Antero Resources (AR) is increasing its production by approximately 20% through its recent transactions and may end up with around 4.2+ Bcfe per day in pro forma 2026 production.

The transactions add around $2 billion to Antero's debt, but it is also expecting close to $500 million in net additional 2026 free cash flow from the transactions. I now project Antero to generate around $1.54 billion in pro forma 2026 free cash flow at the current strip if it goes with a maintenance capex budget.

I have increased my estimate of Antero's value by approximately $1.50 per share to a new estimate of $39 to $40 per share. This is based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Recent Transactions

Antero Resources announced a couple of significant transactions in December 2025. It acquired HG Energy II's upstream assets (West Virginia Marcellus) for $2.8 billion in cash plus the assumption of HG Energy's hedge book. That hedge book is reasonably close to neutral value, although slightly negative at the current strip.

Antero Resources is also divesting its Ohio Utica Shale upstream assets for $800 million in cash.

These transactions are expected to net out to approximately 700 MMCFE per day in additional 2026 production for Antero Resources for a total net cost of $2 billion.

Antero also estimated that its 2026 EBITDAX would increase by approximately $700 million and its 2026 free cash flow would increase by approximately $495 million due to these transactions. This was based on early December 2025 strip prices, which were above $4 for NYMEX natural gas (a bit above the current $3.95 level).

These transactions are a positive for Antero's free cash flow and its future development plans. It is divesting Utica Shale assets that it had paused development on for the last couple of years and adding an estimated five years of Marcellus inventory at maintenance capex levels.

New Unsecured Notes

Antero announced that it was issuing $750 million in 5.4% unsecured notes due 2036 at a price of 99.869% of par. It expects to receive $743 million in net proceeds after expenses and discounts, which it will use to help fund part of its HG acquisition. Antero is also expecting to use the free cash flow from the acquired assets to help pay down its debt.

The 10-year Treasury yield was around 4.1% to 4.2% at the time, so Antero is paying around 1.3% above that. This is also consistent with Antero's BBB- credit rating.

Pro Forma 2026 Outlook

Antero hasn't provided exact details around its expected post-transaction 2026 production mix yet, so I've attempted to model it based on available information. This includes the production mix from its divested Utica assets as well as East Daley's information around HG Energy's production.

Antero may thus average approximately 4.212 Bcfe per day in total 2026 production, pro forma for its recent transactions. This includes 2.7 Bcf per day in natural gas production, for a 36% liquids weighting.

At the current 2026 strip of $3.95 NYMEX natural gas, Antero is projected to generate $6.347 billion in revenues after hedges. This is also net of distributions to Martica and dividends from Antero Midstream.

Antero mentioned a $900 million maintenance capex budget for 2026, including what is needed to maintain the HG Energy volumes.

Antero also highlighted that its cash costs per Mcfe are expected to be reduced by around $0.25 due to the transaction. This is mostly due to lowered processing and transportation costs.

This results in a projection of $1.54 billion in pro forma 2026 free cash flow at current strip prices and a maintenance capex budget.

Antero also mentioned the potential to grow production to 4.4 to 4.5 Bcfe per day in 2027. Notes on Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Antero's value by approximately $1.50 per share. This increases its estimated value to $39 to $40 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario.

While 2026 natural gas strip prices have gone down slightly since I last looked at Antero, its value appears to be enhanced by its transactions. It is selling Utica Shale assets (which were not a development focus) at a healthy multiple to current production and EBITDAX levels. It is adding Marcellus assets at a lower multiple and at a slight discount to PDP plus WIP PV-10.

In addition, Antero anticipates synergies with a PV-10 of $950 million over the next 10 years. This is expected to come from D&C savings and development optimization as well as various other efficiencies.

Conclusion

Antero Resources is expecting around 4.2 Bcfe per day in pro forma 2026 production, up around 20% from Q4 2025 levels due to recent transactions. Antero's net debt is increasing by approximately $2 billion from those deals, but it also expects to generate close to $500 million in additional 2026 free cash flow (on a pro forma basis).

I have increased my estimate of Antero's value after these transactions and now value it at $39 to $40 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment.

data.title: Terrestrial Energy: High-Conviction Play In Nuclear Technology
data.description:
Founded in 2012, Terrestrial Energy (IMSR) is a deep-tech, pre-revenue venture in the nuclear technology space developing the Integral Molten Salt Reactor. IMSR’s business model focuses on commercializing generation IV nuclear technology as small modular reactors (SMRs).

Due to the latest approach in generation IV, IMSR’s nuclear fission technology is considered zero-emission. To produce a fission reaction, IMSR mixes the uranium fuel into liquid molten salt that acts as coolant, allowing the fuel-salt mixture to flow through into the reactor where uranium-splitting happens. Since nothing is getting burned, no CO2 or other gases are produced.

While IMSR’s fission technology produces high-temperature thermal energy, it is also considered to have a decent safety feature due to its fuel-coolant combination:

Inherent safety mechanisms play a critical role in the safety of the IMSR and its commercial value. They draw on the natural behavior of molten salt reactors, which include inherent power control, heat dissipation through convective flow of fuel salt and chemical containment of fission products within the fuel salt. The IMSR is designed to be inherently safe, capturing the many unique safety and distinguishing characteristics of the molten salt reactor technology class.

Source: GlobeNewswire

Since its SPAC in October, share performance has been volatile, hitting a peak near $27 per share before seeing a sharp pullback to reach an all-time low of $6. However, recently, IMSR appears to have seen some renewed optimism as it surges over 60% YTD, largely driven by a key US Department of Energy (DoE) pilot agreement. Currently it is trading around $10.6.

I initiate my coverage with a neutral rating. My 1-year price target of $6.7 suggests a downside from the current level. Nonetheless, it remains hard to value the stock with limited assumptions in my model.

Financial Reviews

As per its first-ever quarterly report release after the SPAC, IMSR remains a pre-commercial company, as in any company developing a novel technology. As it stands, it is probably better to judge IMSR’s performance by looking at its recent contract milestones instead of revenue generation today, in my opinion. To that extent, in my view, the outlook is relatively quite solid for IMSR so far. In particular, the agreement to build and operate a pilot reactor under project TETRA with the DoE represents good progress that could drive federal acceptance and validation.

Beyond the non-operating income from the interests on marketable securities in trust accounts, IMSR effectively has also been unprofitable across the board from an operating standpoint. This is again a normal situation for a lot of deep-tech venture companies with novel technologies. In its first quarter, IMSR saw a -$1.6 million operating loss as it scaled its operations.

As per its first quarterly report, IMSR appears to have decent liquidity, with its current assets, most of which are marketable securities, sitting at over $240 million. In my opinion, though, IMSR’s liquidity could potentially be a critical area in the next twelve months or so, as it needs to balance the operating cash burn with meaningful operating progress. In the nine months ending September 2025, IMSR’s operating cash flow [OCF] loss widened to over -$1 million, almost five times as much as at the same time last year. As the project TETRA unfolds in the next few months, I would expect ISRM to see negative investing cash flow due to a potential increase in CAPEX in the next few quarters as it develops its pilot plant with DoE.

Catalyst

Into 2026, I believe that capturing any opportunities or trends that could help IMSR transform from an ambitious venture into a real commercial energy project could serve as a catalyst for a rating upgrade.

Obviously, the strongest near-term catalyst here would be Project TETRA. In my opinion, if IMSR could execute on this successfully, it would not only allow IMSR to achieve federal validity and regulatory clearance, but also to prove that the complex novel technology works. Overall, I also see that there is a clear macro catalyst being in play here, especially with the US government looking to counter China and Russia’s lead in SMR as geopolitical tension remains high:

There is currently 35GW of generation, or 30 nuclear plants, being constructed in China—more than the rest of the world combined. But China is not just winning in terms of scale; it is leading in advanced technology deployment as well. China’s Linglong-1, or ACP-100, is the world’s first commercial small modular reactor (SMR) that has passed the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) general safety review and is expected to be connected to the grid by the end of 2025. In contrast, the United States has delivered little new nuclear capacity in the last three decades, and its most recent projects, Vogtle Units 3 and 4, faced massive delays and cost overruns.

Source: The Breakthrough Institute

Furthermore, I also think that the recent widespread global AI adoption across consumer and enterprise segments has also driven the need for more data centers with massive energy demand.

Goldman Sachs' report last year in January alone suggested that big tech players have been looking into nuclear energy to meet the energy demand across their new data centers:

Several big tech companies looking for low carbon, round-the-clock energy signed contracts for new nuclear capacity in the last year, and there could be more such deals ahead. Those efforts come as electricity usage by data centers is expected to more than double by 2030, according to reports led by Brian Singer, Jim Schneider, and Carly Davenport. In total, the team forecasts 85-90 gigawatts (GW) of new nuclear capacity would be needed to meet all of the data center power demand growth expected by 2030 (relative to 2023). But well less than 10% will be available globally by 2030.

Source: Goldman Sachs

As such, I believe that any commercial offtake PPA (Power Purchase Agreements), if it were to happen with the likes of Meta, Microsoft, or OpenAI sometime in 2026 or beyond, could not only generate revenue visibility but also significantly unlock positive share re-rating.

Risk

Investing in IMSR means acknowledging its very high risk. High on my list would be the execution and regulation around Project TETRA. I believe that any safety, engineering, or supply chain disruptions during this pilot project could damage IMSR's market narrative and trigger sell-offs.

On the other hand, it appears that the space has gotten quite crowded, presenting real competition from players like Oklo, NuScale, and TerraPower in terms of commercial attention and financial capital. Furthermore, given the differences in technology approaches being used by these competitors, my sense is that whichever player is ahead in terms of regulatory milestones could have an upper hand as a self-proclaimed first mover, potentially blocking others’ technology commercialization pathways in the worst-case scenario.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for IMSR is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions: IMSR sees success in 2026 milestones under the DOE’s Project TETRA. IMSR’s technology becomes the preferred choice by the big techs to power their data centers, securing up to a 1 GW agreement with one of them. In this scenario, I estimated the fair market cap for IMSR by looking at its competitor’s market cap, OKLO, in relation to its project capacity pipeline in terms of GW (gigawatts). Since OKLO has a disclosed pipeline of roughly 14 GW with a market cap of around $14 billion today, suggesting a rough industry standard of $1 billion per 1 GW, then IMSR would have a market cap of around $1 billion. I expect IMSR to maintain the number of implied shares outstanding at 81.77.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - IMSR sees engineering challenges in project TETRA, with construction costs for the pilot plant exceeding estimates, causing the 2026 criticality milestone to be moved further into 2028. In this scenario, I estimate IMSR using its distressed valuation based on how much cash is left into 2026 after it starts investing in Project TETRA. I would assume that IMSR is left with 50% of its total liquidity, which is $121 million, after making a CAPEX investment for the pilot build. This would roughly represent its total assets into 2026. The number of shares will also increase as IMSR raises more money at higher dilution.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $6.7 per share, suggesting that IMSR appears fully-valued and uncertain due to the challenge in properly valuing the stock. At this point, I would give the stock a neutral rating.

Conclusion

In my opinion, IMSR remains a high-conviction, high-risk, and also a high-return play in the SMR space. While the catalysts outlined should help IMSR move from simply a concept to a real deployment with project TETRA, the overall outlook remains unclear. If we zoom out further, competition in the space is intensifying. I continue to rate IMSR’s liquid fuel-coolant technology highly, which has the possibility to be the preferred commercial choice in the future, especially amid the massive energy demand from the growing consumption in upcoming big-tech data centers. However, the thesis will still hinge on a successful 2026 pilot build. My analysis suggests that it remains hard to value IMSR properly under limited assumptions.

data.description:

Arquitos returned 82.1% net of fees and expenses in 2025, compared to 12.8% for the Russell 2000. Individual returns will vary based on the timing of investment. Please check your statement for specific results.

In 2025, Arquitos Capital completed its best year in its 14-year history. We have returned higher than 40% in six of those 14 years.

One year ago, I wrote that I was as confident in the portfolio as I have ever been. While I am pleased by the performance last year, I also believe that our portfolio companies may be just as undervalued today as they were one year ago.

We are primarily concentrated in our top three positions: Liquidia Corporation, ENDI Corp, and Finch Therapeutics. We also have a handful of small positions and some cash. I have been looking at a variety of potential new investments in order to diversify the portfolio, but the good news is that I have not found any that offer a more compelling risk/reward profile than our current holdings.

As a reminder, Arquitos is an unconventional hedge fund invested in a small number of unique companies. I look for company-specific situations where the potential reward meaningfully outweighs the risk. I want multi-bagger potential with limited downside risk.

I am looking for situations where there is a misunderstanding by the market. Typically, this occurs in companies that are going through transitions. There may be structural or strategic changes within the company, including reorganizations, spin-offs, or some other catalyst that is being unlocked. Significant misunderstandings happen more often in smaller companies, so most of my focus is in that area.

These opportunities are fairly rare. That is why the portfolio is concentrated. It can sometimes take years for the public to understand what is happening at the company or for the transition to fully occur. Because of that, short-term performance is not particularly relevant. A company can trade for a depressed price for an extended time period and then have its stock price dramatically increase over a short amount of time once the markets have more clarity.

I have written about this in the past and feel very strongly that we have a significant advantage being comfortable with uncertainty if the downside is protected by a strong balance sheet. Investors love certainty and will pay a premium for it. But often we have opportunities where extremely positive things are happening at a company behind the scenes, but it takes several quarters or years for the income statement to show it. By investing early in those situations, we can capture tremendous upside prior to other shareholders buying in. Then, we can get another leg up in the stock price when those new, more income statement-oriented investors enter.

I have applied this strategy to our three largest positions below:

Liquidia Corporation (LQDA)

Liquidia was our largest contributor in 2025. Shares ended the year at $34.49, increasing from $11.76 at the start of the year. We primarily own long-dated call options that are in the money.

We first bought Liquidia in October 2022 at $4.83 per share. At the time, the company was solely a legal special situation play. Prior to our entry, Liquidia had lost a case at the federal district court level involving the '793 patent owned by competitor United Therapeutics (UTC). However, the Patent Trial and Appeals Board (PTAB) then found that the '793 patent was invalid. This district court decision had been handed down prior to the PTAB decision.

This created an interesting dynamic and extremely attractive entry point. The next step was a PTAB appeal, which Liquidia won. Then, of course, the district court decision had to be overturned since you cannot violate a patent that is not valid.

Since then, we have had several other legal impediments involving Liquidia, UTC, and the Federal Drug Administration. Eventually, in late May 2025, Liquidia’s medication, Yutrepia, finally came to market.

This situation was a sweet spot for the Arquitos strategy. My ideal situation is entering an investment that is a special situation. Due to the uncertainty and complexity, there is a higher chance that the stock is mispriced. Then, when the special situation aspect is resolved and the company performs operationally, a new set of investors who are focused on the income statement begin to invest. This transition involving turnover into different types of investors can create significant opportunities.

Strict special situation investors exit with a nice gain after the legal situation is done, but they miss out on the additional gains (and potential future buyout) that come later. Income statement or growth investors buy from the special situation investors once there is more clarity. These investors missed out on the initial gains but benefit from the longer-term operational success of a company.

My ideal situation is to capture both the special situation gains and the longer-term gains.

Liquidia fit this approach well. Their launch may go down as one of the most successful drug launches ever. They have already taken 25% market share and are effectively expanding the market. Amazingly, 75% of all patients starting on Yutrepia are new to treprostinil.

As a reminder, treprostinil is the generic drug delivered either through inhaled methods, oral, via IV or subcutaneous. Liquidia’s treatment method is more effective than their competitor's because of Liquidia’s patented PRINT manufacturing process. PRINT allows the treprostinil powder to be smaller and in a more uniform shape, allowing the drug to reach the deep lung in patients. This both increases effectiveness and reduces side effects like cough. Many patients have a very difficult time breathing, and Yutrepia’s low effort inhaler is much easier for them. The competitor’s manufacturing process does not allow for the use of a low-effort inhaler.

Other delivery methods for treprostinil carry their own side effects and are used at different stages of disease progression. It is generally better for the patient to be on the inhaled treatment as long as side effects are lower and high enough doses of treprostinil can be delivered to the deep lung. This is why clinical studies involving Yutrepia show how effective it is for patients. Yutrepia can deliver higher doses with fewer side effects. Liquidia should be able to capture, and already are capturing, the inhaled market, naïve patient market, and some of those patients on other treatment regimens.

Specific to the inhaled market, CEO Roger Jeffs believes that Liquidia can take 80% to 90% of UTC’s market share. Liquidia’s Q4 results show that this is beginning to happen. Liquidia generated $30 million in positive free cash flow in Q4 2025 on $90 million in revenue. That number will exponentially rise from here.

The final significant legal issue is a case where UTC alleged that Liquidia violated UTC’s ‘327 patent. That patent generally covers improving exercise capacity in patients suffering from PH-ILD. This patent and case do not involve the treatment of patients suffering from PAH.

The trial was in late June 2025. We are expecting a decision any day. The outcome could range from a full win by Liquidia to a full removal of Liquidia’s ability to have PH-ILD listed on Yutrepia’s labeling. There also could be potential outcomes in between, such as some sort of royalty involving PH-ILD prescriptions. Liquidia is confident in a full win, but anything can happen in litigation. Many legal observers believe that the longer it has taken for the judge to provide his decision, the better it is for Liquidia.

Importantly, Liquidia has stated that 75% of their revenue so far is from PAH prescriptions. So, even in a worst-case scenario involving this litigation, the company’s potential is still strong. We are likely to see some volatility, either positive or negative, depending on the outcome. If the decision goes against Liquidia, I believe the share price decline would be short-lived, as their PAH business alone supports a higher stock price than today.

The company recently presented at the JP Morgan healthcare conference on January 14. They provided details on the launch of Yutrepia, the potential for their next generation nebulizer, L606, and a host of new clinical trials to show that Yutrepia can also effectively treat Pulmonary Fibrosis, Systemic Sclerosis associated with Raynaud’s Phenomenon, and COPD. These markets dwarf the current PAH and PH-ILD market that Liquidia participates in.

In the second half of 2026, Liquidia is participating in an investigator-initiated study to confirm the effectiveness of combining sotatercept with treprostinil for better patient outcomes. Sotatercept is an injection that reduces lung vessel pressure, while inhaled treprostinil reduces long vessel resistance. This combination therapy has the potential to become the gold standard for PAH patients. The commercial name for sotatercept is Winrevair. It is owned by Merck. Merck has been active in the M&A space, and many believe that they would be a logical buyer of Liquidia.

At the JP Morgan conference, Roger Jeffs said that he believed Liquidia would reach $1 billion in revenue in 2027. I believe that number is conservative. In early 2024, I said that I thought Liquidia’s base case was $75 per share by 2027, with a bull case of $100 per share. If the company generates the $1 billion in revenue that Roger Jeffs believes, shares should trade above that bull case number.

ENDI Corp. (ENDI)

One year ago, I wrote that ENDI was the best near-term risk/reward opportunity since I launched Arquitos Capital. Shares began 2025 trading at $11.43, which was less than 5x EBITDA net of cash and investments. Assets Under Management (AUM) were at $3.4 billion, and the annual EBITDA run rate was $8 million. ENDI’s stock was cheap on its own and did not give credit for any future growth. The chances of losing money on an investment with those characteristics and at that price were minuscule.

The stock rose to $16.75 by year-end. The EBITDA run rate hit $12.5 million annually, and AUM stood at $4.1 billion. Using the last publicly reported financial numbers, ENDI continues to trade around 5x EBITDA. Similar specialized asset managers trade at valuations far above that number.

A major overhang for the stock was the outstanding warrants at the company level. These were issued when ENDI acquired CrossingBridge. The strike price was $8 per share. The accounting treatment for these warrants made the income statement confusing and especially difficult for new potential shareholders analyzing the company for the first time.

The company did an early cashless exercise of these warrants (at a discount) prior to the end of 2025. This means that the financial statements in the future will be more clear and the company will be easier to analyze. Now that the market cap is above $100 million and the structure is cleaner, I expect the company will begin to attract additional interest and become more fairly valued over time.

Finch Therapeutics (FNCH)

Finch shares ended 2025 at $13.49, rising by 19% for the year despite no major updates to their court case.

Finch won a jury trial in August 2024 against Ferring Pharmaceuticals, with the jury finding that Ferring infringed on three of Finch’s patents. The jury awarded Finch approximately $30 million in a one-time licensing fee and pre-trial interest, plus ongoing royalties to be determined by the judge and post-trial interest. Importantly, the jury also found willful infringement, opening the door for enhanced damages up to three times the jury award.

We continue to wait for the post-trial decision from the judge, where she will determine an ongoing royalty rate and determine whether enhanced damages will be awarded. My guess is that part of the delay is that the parties are attempting to negotiate a settlement. Patience is our friend here, as interest on the judgement accrues at the Federal Reserve discount rate plus 5%.

I expect our administrator to deliver the K-1s in late March.

For our audit, you may receive communications and verification requests from Wipfli as they perform their work. The sooner you complete and return these items, the sooner we can receive the fund’s final audit. I appreciate your cooperation. If you have any questions about any of these items, please let me know.

Thank you again for your commitment to Arquitos, and best wishes for 2026!

Best regards,

Steven Kiel

Disclaimer

This letter is for informational purposes only and does not reflect all of the positions bought, sold, or held by Arquitos Capital Offshore Master, Ltd. or its feeder funds, Arquitos Capital Partners, LP, and Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd. Any performance data is historical in nature and is not an indication of future results. All investments involve risk, including the loss of principal. We disclaim any duty to provide updates or changes to the information contained in this letter.

Performance returns presented above are for Arquitos Capital Partners, LP and reflect the fund’s total return, net of fees and expenses, since its April 10, 2012, inception. They are net of the high water mark and the 20% performance fee, applied after a 4% hurdle, as detailed in the confidential private offering memorandum. Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd. was launched on March 1, 2018. Returns from Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd. may differ slightly and are not presented here.

Performance returns for 2025 are estimated by our third-party administrator, pending the year-end audit. Actual returns may differ from the returns presented. Positions reflected in this letter do not represent all the positions held, purchased, or sold.

This letter in no way constitutes an offer or solicitation to buy an interest in Arquitos Capital Partners, LP, Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd., or any of Arquitos Capital Management’s other funds or affiliates. Such an offer may only be made pursuant to the delivery of an approved confidential private offering memorandum to an investor.

data.title: Gold Rally Hits $4,900: Why The January FOMC, Geopolitics, And Technical Warnings Mark A Critical Juncture
data.description:
By Moheb Hanna

The historic rally in gold prices, which saw the metal surge past $4,900 per ounce in late 2025 and early 2026, was driven by a rare convergence of macroeconomic and geopolitical factors. With the January 27-28, 2026 FOMC meeting approaching, the backdrop of high-altitude stability for gold is in focus. Prices have already surged significantly - trading near $4,800-5,000 - driven by a combination of fiscal expansion, geopolitical friction, and institutional uncertainty. The January meeting is less about a "rate move" and more about the "narrative shift."

How this meeting is poised to impact gold prices

The "Pause" and interest rate path

According to the CME FedWatch tool, the futures markets are pricing in just under two 25 bps interest rate cuts for 2026, with the cut expectations mostly for the second half of 2026. Lower nominal rates reduce the "opportunity cost" of holding gold, which pays no interest, making it more competitive against Treasury bonds.

For the January 27-28, 2026, FOMC meeting, the market consensus is that the Fed will hold rates steady at 3.50-3.75% after a string of cuts in late 2025. The CME FedWatch tool has 95% of participants expecting the Fed to hold, meaning a pause is largely priced in. However, gold’s sensitivity to the "Neutral Rate" remains the key variable. If Chair Powell’s press conference suggests that 3.5% is the new floor (the terminal rate) due to resilient growth, gold could see a short-term "profit-taking" pullback. Conversely, if the Fed expresses concern over the cooling labor market (currently adding only 50k jobs/month), markets will pull forward expectations for a June cut, providing a tailwind for non-yielding bullion.

Inflation vs. fiscal policy

Concerns around stagflation dominate the 2026 economic landscape. Inflation remains stubbornly above the 2% target, remaining near 2.7-2.8%, while expansionary fiscal policies and tax cuts are propping up growth.

Historically, gold has thrived when the Fed was perceived as "behind the curve." If the FOMC statement acknowledges that inflation progress has stalled but refuses to hike rates (due to growth risks), it reinforces gold's status as the ultimate inflation hedge.

Institutional independence and geopolitical risk

Unusually for an FOMC meeting, non-monetary factors are heavily influencing the "Fed premium" in gold prices. The recent legal and political challenges to the Fed’s independence (including the Supreme Court's scrutiny of Governor Lisa Cook's removal and investigations into Chair Powell) have fueled concerns about the Fed’s independence and its potential impact on future interest rates. Investors are using gold as a hedge against a potential "politicization" of the dollar. Any rhetoric in the January 28 statement that hints at defensiveness toward the Fed’s autonomy could actually spike gold prices as a vote of no confidence in the USD. Major banks (Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan) have already raised 2026 targets to $5,000-5,400, suggesting that as long as the Fed doesn't pivot back to aggressive hiking, the path of least resistance for gold remains upward.

With all that said, will this price rally continue? We will go over different technical analysis tools and indicators, as well as the most recent COT report released on January 16th, 2026, including data up to January 13, 2026

Commitments of Traders (COT) Report

Managed money (Large speculators): Trend followers

Unlike previous peaks where speculators were "overextended," the current net-long level is not yet at a historical sentiment extreme. Although this suggests there is more room to the upside regarding long positions, and they haven't yet reached the "exhaustion" point that usually precedes a major crash, a negative divergence may be in play as price makes higher highs while long positions are plotting lower highs.

Swap dealers: Market makers and hedgers

In contrast to the managed money category, swap dealers, who typically take the other side of the market, have already reached their all-time extreme short level, suggesting a change in sentiment may be due.

​Small speculators: Growing participation

There is a noticeable uptick in net long positions from small speculators in early 2026. This indicates that "Main Street" investors are increasingly entering the market, often in the final stage of a significant price surge.

Open interest and volume

Although open interest remains high, it is showing a potential negative divergence with price action. Similar to OI, non-trending low volume is also seen along with price appreciation as of November 1, 2025, which theoretically means that volume was not supportive of the price increase since November 1.

Technical analysis: Gold daily chart - Bullish momentum testing resistance

  • The daily chart shows a strong, long-term bullish trend that accelerated significantly in 2026. After a period of consolidation in late 2025, the price has broken out to the upside, currently trading near 4,900.00.
  • As of early 2025, the gold price traded within a widening, rising channel, as indicated by the blue lines on the chart above. The "higher highs and higher lows" structure is well-defined, with the upper channel boundary intersecting with monthly R3 at 4913.58, forming a confluence of resistance.
  • As of late October 2025, following a negative divergence with the RSI that preceded a $200 drop, gold prices rose back up, erasing the drop, breaking above its fast EMA9, the monthly PP of $4345.22, and the baseline of multiple higher lows, as marked by the red lines on the chart.
  • A confluence of support lies beneath the price action, represented by the intersection of the EMA9, the breakout level (red line), and the newly formed price gap near $4630.00. The price gap may suggest an island formation; however, that’s yet to be determined.
  • RSI (14) is currently at 78.00, indicating the asset is in overbought territory. A potential negative divergence remains between price and RSI.
  • ​Stochastic (14, 1, 3): The Stochastic oscillator is also highly elevated (87.90), confirming strong bullish momentum but suggesting that a short-term “cooling-off” period or minor retracement toward the EMA may be imminent.
