data.title: ENFR: My Choice In The Midstream, And Some Drivers Not To Underestimate
data.description:
When I think of a sector that has regained momentum, I think of midstream energy, and among the ETFs that try to represent it, personally, I really appreciate Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR).

I notice a strong relationship between the performance of the ETF’s holdings and credit/monetary dynamics, almost more than energy ones. The reason? In my opinion, its composition made up of C-corps and MLP entails steady cash flows but pronounced costs of capital. If the latter declines, then the market can also allow itself to expand company valuations. But before getting into these details …

What is ENFR

We are talking about a fund with a primary objective of price + yield of the index and as a secondary objective, total return (income + capital appreciation). To do this, ENFR replicates the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index (AMEI), an index that cannot exactly be defined as a “pure MLP ETF.” Unlike classic indices such as the Alerian MLP Index, here there is a strong presence of C-Corps (such as Kinder Morgan, Enbridge, etc.). This makes ENFR more of a midstream hybrid: in detail, it has an MLP limit of 25% of total assets; this means that about 75% is in C-corps.

Participating involves an expense ratio of 0.35% (very low for the category it belongs to) and a median bid/ask spread of 0.19%, with a relatively contained turnover of 33%. Since we are talking about costs, it makes sense to remember that ENFR is an RIC (Regulated Investment Company), which (at least at a theoretical level) entails lower tax drag and greater NAV retention. Which (again theoretically) places it on an interesting level for taxable accounts and IRAs.

Distribution

The fund has a quarterly distribution, currently corresponding to a TTM distribution of 4.53% with a 30-day SEC yield of 5.04%. A distribution that is not as pronounced as classic MLPs but still steady and linear (an element that I personally appreciate a lot). All of this (naturally) with a very high Return of Capital of ~53.3% of the TTM distribution (also justified by its tax nature).

This is also partly due to the business model of the companies that make up the ETF, which is based on fee-based contracts and relatively stable cash flows. In fact, let’s look specifically at which companies we are talking about.

Holding Distribution

We are talking about 27 companies, with a strong concentration in the top 10 holdings equal to 60% of a total capitalization of $755 bn.

Of this distribution of holdings, about 71% is in the USA, the rest in Canada, all large cap (total absence of small cap). At a sector level, we are talking about companies active in the North American energy infrastructure segment, but with market characteristics close to those of utilities. As you well know, there is a tight connection here.

This clearly results in a hidden concentration on Natural Gas, which, in my opinion, cannot be completely ignored (gas pipelines and gathering & process), close to 60%. A commodity by which it is naturally (partly) influenced, especially in short-term fluctuations.

It also has a 28% exposure to oil pipelines, which seems to be scaring some investors a bit due to new bearish expectations on the price of oil, but which, in my opinion, is not that influential.

Peers

ENFR has a beta practically equal to 1 versus the AMEI index, but it has an alpha of -0.6 due to cost frictions and replication techniques that increase tracking error. However, it remains very representative in terms of the beta of the segment it aims to represent.

In this sense, the comparison with the MLP Index is mandatory: here the beta versus MLP drops to 0.75. And moving to the alpha side, two elements stand out in my opinion: a lower upside capture by ENFR, equal to 90, but a downside capture of 72. So, in this sense, it is a more conservative solution. From here derives the comparison with AMLP, which uses the MLP index as a benchmark.

At first glance, an important difference in the TER immediately stands out, with ENFR clearly more cost-effective. Going further into detail, a divergence in distributions also emerges: by reducing exposure to the MLP component, ENFR ends up giving up part of the income stream. It is precisely from here that, in my view, the performance gap that emerged in the period following 2022 originates. Businesses that are heavily asset-heavy and highly sensitive to the cost of capital, refinancing dynamics, and credit spreads tend to suffer during phases of financial tightening. ENFR, with a greater presence of C-corps, therefore showed greater weakness in a credit-tightening environment such as the post-2022 period. If this interpretation is correct, then it is natural to expect that in a phase of credit stabilization, ENFR can gradually close the gap relative to AMLP. A dynamic that has indeed already manifested itself quite clearly starting from 2023.

My Opinion

After the end of the Chevron doctrine + a long series of Trump’s executive orders, the sector’s focus has shifted from revenues to the implicit cost of capital. This, together with credit and monetary dynamics, acts on the denominator (WACC) and seems to allow a justified expansion of valuation. This, considering long, predictable, contract-based cash flows in a context of ever-increasing energy demand.

How to Position

Personally, I therefore remain positive on this sector, and I look with interest at this phase of potential accumulation that started around $30. Being a sector with positive momentum, I orient myself using the 14-period RSI strategy on the 30 level, and in ENFR I see a potential controlled-risk trade: the fund distributes more than an average 4% per year and has a POC around the $30 area about 12% away (to the downside).

Therefore, considering the positive impact of the compression in the cost of capital, prospectively also supported by liquidity injections into the credit system by Trump, and the prospects of Fed cuts, the market could price in a further reduction in the cost of capital on ENFR’s holdings, which, with FCF unchanged, would further raise the aggregate target price.

For this reason, I remain bullish on ENFR at price lows (technical side).

Risk

I want to clarify one thing: in my opinion, attention should go beyond concerns about gas or oil in recent months. The risk develops in two directions:

  • Regulatory dynamics are fragile: they change depending on consensus. Therefore, in my opinion, they are not such strong drivers in the long term.
  • Stable, or moderately high, real rates for a long time still; and considering that even today the market is pricing in high yields, this means that market participants do not strongly believe in the idea of new rate cuts, or in any case they expect higher inflation in the future as a result of this decision. And if this direction were to continue, the thesis that justifies an expansion of target prices, making growth less sustainable, would fall apart.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think the right rating for ENFR is BUY. In my opinion, it has a structure that revolves around an index built in a competitive way compared even to the classic MLP. I am not concerned by the fact that distributions are halved, nor that 75% is made up of C-corps. I think credit prospects can have a positive impact and leave room for an expansion in ENFR’s valuations. For this reason, I still look with interest at the lows.

data.title: Canadian REIT Capital Offerings Up Over 23% In 2025
data.description:
Editor's note: This article is published quarterly with current data available at that time.

Publicly traded Canadian real estate investment trusts raised C$6.23 billion through capital offerings in 2025, an increase of more than 23% from the C$5.03 billion collected a year earlier.

Debt offerings accounted for C$5.44 billion of capital raised, and common equity offerings brought in about C$785.7 million.

Retail REITs account for 60% of total capital

Six property sectors in Canada raised capital in 2025. The retail sector, comprising shopping centers and other retail properties, accounted for the biggest share at C$3.77 billion, or more than 60% of the total for the year.

The office segment came in second, collecting C$850.0 million, or 13.7% of total capital raised. The multifamily sector ranked third at C$561.9 million, about 9.0% of the total.

The healthcare and industrial sectors collected C$500.0 million each, while the diversified sector raised C$40.3 million.

Allied Properties pulls in the most capital

The single largest common equity offering in 2025 was multifamily-focused GO Residential REIT's (GO.U) C$561.9 million IPO completed July 25. The REIT used the net proceeds from the offering in large part to fund the acquisition of the initial portfolio.

Office landlord Allied Properties REIT (APYRF) attracted the most capital overall in 2025 through three debt offerings totaling C$850.0 million. Its C$450.0 million offering, which wrapped up Feb. 24, 2025, was the largest debt issuance completed during the year. Part of the offering's net proceeds were intended to finance or refinance eligible green projects and fully repay the construction loan on 19 Duncan St. in Toronto.

Allied Properties' two other offerings were completed April 7, 2025: C$250.0 million of 4.312% series M senior unsecured debentures and C$150.0 million of floating rate series L senior unsecured debentures. Both series of debentures are due April 7, 2027.

Other retail REIT Choice Properties REIT (PPRQF) and shopping center REITs SmartCentres REIT (CWYUF) and First Capital REIT (FCXXF) all tied for the second spot, each raising C$800.0 million in 2025 through debt offerings. Shopping center-focused RioCan REIT (RIOCF) followed next with three debt offerings totaling C$750.0 million.

Capital offerings up over 12% sequentially in Q4 2025

Following a jump in the third quarter, capital market activity continued to increase during the fourth quarter of 2025, bringing in C$1.42 billion for Canadian REITs. The total represented a hike of more than 12% from the previous quarter; however, it was a drop of more than 16% from the C$1.70 billion raised during the fourth quarter of 2024.

During the fourth quarter of 2025, shopping center REIT Primaris REIT (PMREF) raised C$168.9 million through a follow-on common equity offering. The REIT said it would use the proceeds to pay down debt relating to the recently completed acquisition of Promenades Saint-Bruno.

Single-tenant-focused Automotive Properties REIT (APPTF) completed a C$38.9 million follow-on common equity offering Oct. 23, 2025. The retail REIT said it intended to use a portion of the proceeds to repay indebtedness under its credit facilities, including the debt incurred to fund the cash portion of the acquisition of three automotive dealership properties in Dorval, Quebec.

Automotive Properties collected C$16.0 million through another common equity offering also completed Oct. 23.

SmartCentres and First Capital pulled in the biggest amounts of capital during the fourth quarter, both at C$500.0 million.

During the fourth quarter, SmartCentres sold $250.0 million of 3.599% series AC senior unsecured debentures due June 12, 2029, and $250.0 million of 4.318% series AD senior unsecured debentures due June 12, 2032. It intended to use the net proceeds to refinance existing debt and for general corporate purposes.

First Capital raised C$250.0 million through its 4.760% series G senior unsecured debentures due Feb. 15, 2035. The shopping REIT used the proceeds to repay, in part, the early redemption of C$300 million of outstanding co-issued 3.604% series T senior unsecured debentures due May 6, 2026, at the end of December 2025. The REIT also collected another C$250.0 million through its 4.461% series F senior unsecured debentures due Feb. 15, 2034, and intended to use the proceeds to repay existing debt and for general business purposes.

Meanwhile, RioCan sold C$200.0 million of 4.417% series AP senior unsecured debentures due Oct. 1, 2032. The shopping center REIT said it would use the net proceeds for debt repayment and general business purposes.

data.title: TE Connectivity: A Booming AI Business Lifts Spirits
data.description:
In the spring of last year, I concluded that TE Connectivity (TEL) was resorting to M&A to improve its growth and positioning following a +$2 billion deal for Richards Manufacturing.

The focus on M&A came as the company had not really lived up to its promises for sales growth, even as the positioning of the business is generally fine, and TE has a strong long-term track record. While valuations look very reasonable at a high-teens earnings multiple, I was not too impressed with ongoing growth, making me cautious.

This stance has been too cautious, as shares have risen by nearly 60% over the past year. This has been supported by strong business momentum as well as a substantial re-rating, as organic growth improved meaningfully, notably thanks to an exploding AI business.

However, part of the gains were driven by a valuation re-rating, for good reasons. With the booming AI-related business making up just about 15% of growth and the remainder of the business posting much more muted growth, I am remaining cautious, waiting for better entry points.

All About Connection

TE Connectivity describes its business as a leading industrial technology business that focuses on a +$100 billion connectivity market. The company generates some $17 billion in revenues from such solutions, with revenues split pretty evenly between the industrial and transportation segments.

Here, the company provides connectivity and sensor solutions used to create a safer, sustainable, and connected future. Such solutions are provided in quite harsh environments, including digital data networks, energy, aerospace & defense, automated & connected living, medical, automotive, sensor, and commercial transportation.

The company has operations spanning over a hundred countries, with over a hundred manufacturing sites present around the globe as well. This provides real diversification on top of diversification founded in end markets, with the company being a large business, employing over 80,000 employees.

The company has quite a solid track record, having grown sales from about $11 billion to $18 billion over the past decade, although that revenue flatlined around $16 billion in the period 2022-2024. GAAP operating margins improved quite a few points, up to levels around 20% of sales, as another big achievement has been 20% of the shares being bought back over this period of time.

A $60 stock a decade ago rose to the $150 mark last spring, as shares have been on fire over the past year, having risen to a high of $250 per share, now trading at $230 per share.

Some Perspective

As alluded to before, the situation around TE was not that bright early last year. This came after the company reported a 1% fall in fiscal 2024 sales to $15.8 billion, driven by general weakness. Despite this weakness, earnings improved, with adjusted earnings reported at $7.56 per share, as a net debt load of $2.9 billion was pretty manageable.

Trading at $150 in March, shares traded at 19-20 times earnings, with few immediate green shoots seen. The company posted flattish first-quarter results. Valued at $48 billion at $150 per share, the company announced a substantial bolt-on deal with a $2.3 billion deal for Richards Manufacturing, a rather expensive deal valued at 6 times sales, while all of TE traded around 3 times sales.

Pro forma net debt was seen increasing above $5 billion, yet I pegged leverage ratios only around 1.5 times EBITDA. Working with an $8 per share number, a resulting 18-19 times forward earnings multiple looks pretty reasonable for a fine and modestly leveraged business, as real execution seemed to be lacking here.

Improvements Arrive

In March, TE Connectivity reported a 9% increase in the quarterly dividend, now payable at $0.71 per share each quarter. Soon momentum improved, with second-quarter organic growth accelerating to 5%, as the order intake was up as much as 6%.

Third-quarter sales rose by 14%, driven by the Richards deal, yet a 9% organic growth rate should not be underestimated as well. This momentum only accelerated with fourth-quarter sales reported up as much as 17%, with organic growth accelerating to 11%.

Strong momentum resulted in full-year sales reported up 9% to $17.3 billion, with organic growth up 6%. However, the momentum was really different this time, with transportation solution sales down around a percent for the year, while industrial sales were up over 23%. This, in its turn, was driven by digital data networks and energy markets.

All this drove some margin gains as well, with adjusted earnings up a dollar and twenty cents to $8.76 per share. Net debt was down to $4.4 billion, driven by retained earnings since the Richard deal, with leverage far from being an issue.

With this trend firmly in place, the company guided for another 17% increase in first-quarter sales, with ongoing growth still seen in the double digits, increasing by around 11%.

Just Keeps Going

In January, the company reported a 22% increase in first-quarter sales to $4.7 billion, with organic growth of 15% outpacing the outlook by around four points. Adjusted earnings rose by a third to $2.72 per share, with earnings trending around $11 per share. Moreover, continued momentum is seen in a $5.1 billion order intake, adding about $400 million to a non-quantified backlog, as net debt is pretty stable at $4.4 billion amidst continued buybacks.

The growth acceleration comes as transportation solutions are now seeing solid growth as well, while industrial growth approaches 40% on an annual basis. This, in its turn, is driven by nearly 70% growth in the digital data network business, which is now a nearly $3 billion business on an annual basis.

The company guided for slower growth, in part because of tougher comparables, with second-quarter sales seen up 13%, driven by 6% organic growth. Anticipated adjusted earnings of $2.65 per share are actually seen down a few pennies on a sequential basis. This likely is the reason shares have been trading stagnantly since the fall, in fact, down about 8% from the highs.

What Now?

Trading at $231 per share and earnings power having jumped from about $8 per share to about $11 per share, TE has seen dramatic earnings improvements as well as a re-rating in the share price. The earnings multiple has jumped from 18-19 times last year to about 22 times here, as the company has been unleashed again.

Frankly, the results are impressive, with organic sales increasing by double-digit percentages, but the fact that this was flat just a year ago shows the volatility herein and reliance on the AI boom to drive growth here. Moreover, the transportation business remains a dominant business, one that I have lower regard for, as the re-rating is really driven by the industrial businesses and, in particular, the AI business.

Frankly, the company has delivered, driven by the AI boom, but this growth is more widespread. Shares are not necessarily super expensive, but a shortfall in this business, or somewhat poorer-positioned transportation business, makes me cautious here, as expectations have risen substantially.

With the second quarter guidance softening, which very well might turn out to be conservative, the issue is that I am not willing to chase shares here, certainly as the growth guidance is softening a bit. This makes me cautiously optimistic, and I am not yet willing to get involved. However, upon further developments or a pullback to the $200 mark, I am pleased to revisit this cautious yet constructive stance.
